tweets.text:
Now today was a proper liquidation day. I didn't know if it came all at once or bled slowly over the next week (like 2018, bled for 2-weeks).

Let the burden of proof show that this MOU can reverse the deficit.

Note: November 5, 2018 saw Trump grant extensions on the Iranian oil sanctions that led to one of the largest speculator positioning washouts in oil market history. 

Oil inventories did not decline during this period.

tweets.text: This makes complete sense, as the Citi Surprise gauge is just a measure of whether economic data exceed economists' expectations.  Economists tend to be slow to adjust their outlooks in response to actual economic changes.  It suggests we could be in for a bit of rough slogging coming up, though.

tweets.text:
“Translating the observed ~4.6 mb/d cumulative inventory draw over this period using the historical beta between inventory changes and market imbalance implies an underlying deficit of roughly 7.3 mb/d on a seasonally adjusted basis.”

“Even if the Strait normalizes by month-end, we expect this tightening trend to persist at least through July. Moreover, unlike in 2022, current US SPR releases are structured as loans rather than outright supply additions.”

“This raises a key question: with commercial inventories entering peak demand season at
historically tight levels and the cyclical demand impulse the strongest since 2022, why should prices not be materially higher? Around 60% of oil demand is tied to the production and movement of goods. While a gradual easing toward $80/b Brent by end-2027 appears plausible, we see near-term risks to prices as skewed to the upside.

One potential justification for current pricing is the assumption that war-related inventory
losses will be rapidly reversed as supply recovers faster than demand. While possible, we see this as unlikely. Rerouting shipping, clearing logistical bottlenecks, and restarting production will take time, and the risk of renewed flare-ups and further supply disruptions remains non-trivial.

Last, our demand estimates for China are on the conservative side. Based on the preliminary estimate for May, we estimate China's oil demand fell 200 kb/d y/y on average during the first five months of the year, but our forecast is a 370 kb/d decline for the full year, assuming the Strait normalizes by the end of this month. If the current capex cycle largely holds, we would
expect a strong rebound in Chinese demand.”

Barclays

“Translating the observed ~4.6 mb/d cumulative inventory draw over this period using the historical beta between inventory changes and market imbalance implies an underlying deficit of roughly 7.3 mb/d on a seasonally adjusted basis.”

“Even if the Strait normalizes by month-end, we expect this tightening trend to persist at least through July. Moreover, unlike in 2022, current US SPR releases are structured as loans rather than outright supply additions.”

“This raises a key question: with commercial inventories entering peak demand season at
historically tight levels and the cyclical demand impulse the strongest since 2022, why should prices not be materially higher? Around 60% of oil demand is tied to the production and movement of goods. While a gradual easing toward $80/b Brent by end-2027 appears plausible, we see near-term risks to prices as skewed to the upside.

One potential justification for current pricing is the assumption that war-related inventory
losses will be rapidly reversed as supply recovers faster than demand. While possible, we see this as unlikely. Rerouting shipping, clearing logistical bottlenecks, and restarting production will take time, and the risk of renewed flare-ups and further supply disruptions remains non-trivial.

Last, our demand estimates for China are on the conservative side. Based on the preliminary estimate for May, we estimate China's oil demand fell 200 kb/d y/y on average during the first five months of the year, but our forecast is a 370 kb/d decline for the full year, assuming the Strait normalizes by the end of this month. If the current capex cycle largely holds, we would
expect a strong rebound in Chinese demand.”
